title: Clinical Documentation Assistant
tagline: Turn doctor dictation into structured notes — without the AI ever guessing a diagnosis or a dose.

levels.label: Junior
levels.intro: Get it working: setup, persona, structured output, error handling.
levels.theory: A clinical documentation assistant takes a doctor's spoken or typed encounter notes and turns them into a structured note (commonly SOAP: Subjective, Objective, Assessment, Plan). The model is a summariser and formatter, not a diagnostician — the system prompt must say this explicitly, because nothing else stops the model from inventing impressions or treatment plans from a vague transcript. Use genai.Client(api_key=...) with the key loaded via load_dotenv() from a .env file, never hardcoded. Ask for structured output (e.g. response_mime_type 'application/json' with a defined schema) rather than free text — a doctor's tooling needs to parse Subjective/Objective/Assessment/Plan into separate fields reliably, and free text makes that fragile. Every response must be wrapped in error handling: a malformed or empty model response should produce a visible 'note generation failed' flag, never a silently blank or partially-filled clinical note.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Junior theory — specifically the part about the model being a summariser, not a diagnostician, and why that has to be a system_instruction rule, not an assumption.

levels.label: Senior
levels.intro: Make it efficient: timeouts, retries, validation, never caching across patients.
levels.theory: Two encounters never produce the same transcript, so caching model output by content hash — the pattern you'd use for a general chatbot — is actively dangerous here: a near-identical transcript from a different patient must never reuse a cached note. Instead, focus efficiency work on: (1) Timeouts — a hung API call during a live consultation blocks the doctor's workflow, so every call needs a hard timeout with a clear 'try again' fallback. (2) Retry with backoff on transient failures only (timeouts, 5xx) — never retry on a validation failure, since that means the model's output was the problem, not the network. (3) Schema validation before anything touches the patient record — if the model returns JSON missing a required field (e.g. no Assessment section), the note must be rejected and flagged for manual completion, not silently saved with an empty field that looks intentional.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Senior theory on why this pipeline must never cache by content — and what's supposed to happen when the model's JSON is missing a required field.

levels.label: Architect
levels.intro: Make it safe: PII handling, hallucination guardrails, audit logging.
levels.theory: Three architect-level risks are specific to clinical tooling. (1) PII — transcripts contain names, dates of birth, and identifiers. Whatever can be stripped or tokenised before the data leaves your infrastructure should be, and what must reach the model should be logged separately from any third-party API logs. (2) Hallucinated specifics — LLMs can produce a confident-sounding drug dose, lab value, or differential diagnosis that was never in the transcript. The system prompt must forbid inventing any numeric value (doses, vitals, lab results) not explicitly present in the source text, and the model should output 'not stated' rather than filling a gap. (3) Audit logging — every generated note needs a stored record of exactly what transcript produced it and which model/version generated it, because in clinical contexts you need to reconstruct exactly what happened if a note is later questioned.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Architect theory on hallucinated specifics — the rule is 'not stated', not a best guess.

levels.label: Executive
levels.intro: Decide if it's worth running: human-in-the-loop, liability, launch readiness.
levels.theory: At the Executive level the central decision isn't technical, it's a liability and workflow question: this tool drafts, it does not finalise. Every generated note must require explicit doctor sign-off before it becomes part of the patient record — the AI removes typing time, not clinical responsibility. Cost scales with transcript length and visit volume, not user count, so the biggest lever is trimming transcript noise (filler, repeated phrasing) before it ever reaches the model, the same instinct as compressing dictated text elsewhere. Launch readiness means: sign-off step is mandatory and cannot be bypassed in the UI, PII handling has been reviewed against the relevant regulatory framework (e.g. HIPAA/GDPR depending on jurisdiction), and there's a clear, logged path to override or discard any note the doctor doesn't trust.
levels.theory_hint: Re-read the Executive theory — the tool drafts, the doctor decides. That's the one fact that should never be negotiable in the design.

tasks.scenario: What is the ONE rule that must be in the system prompt of a clinical documentation assistant, before anything else?
options: Always produce a confident, complete-sounding note even if the transcript is vague — doctors prefer a finished draft over a gappy one
The model summarises and formats only — it must never infer a diagnosis, dose, or value that wasn't explicitly stated in the transcript
Use the most medically sophisticated language possible so the note reads as professionally as possible
Default to the most common diagnosis for the symptoms described, since it's statistically the most likely
hints: Think about what happens the first time a doctor reads a confident-sounding sentence the model invented from a vague transcript.
Only one option keeps the model strictly bounded to what's actually in the source text.
tasks.why: A documentation assistant's entire value depends on the note reflecting only what was said. The moment it fills gaps with plausible-sounding content, every note becomes something a doctor has to fact-check line by line — which defeats the purpose of automating it.

tasks.scenario: A doctor's clinic wants to test the note generator on their own desktop before rolling it out across the practice. It works fine on the hosted demo, but on the clinic machine transcript_client.api_key is always None and note generation fails immediately. Find the missing line.

checks.label: Calls load_dotenv() before reading the environment

tasks.why: The hosted demo has GEMINI_API_KEY set directly in its server environment, so os.environ.get() finds it without any extra step. The clinic desktop has no such setup — the key only lives in a local .env file next to the script, and os.environ.get() never reads .env files by itself. load_dotenv() from python-dotenv has to run first to copy that file into the process environment. Without it, the key is silently None on the clinic machine, and generate_note() fails on every dictation — right when a doctor is trying to evaluate whether the tool is worth adopting.

tasks.scenario: Write the system prompt for a clinical note generator. It must define the assistant's role AND include an explicit rule about never inventing a value (dose, vital, diagnosis) that wasn't in the transcript.

checks.label: Defines a summarising/formatting role, not a diagnosing one
Has an explicit rule against inventing unstated values

tasks.why: The system prompt needs two things: a role that scopes the model to summarising, and a hard rule against filling gaps. Without the second part, the first part alone doesn't stop the model from being 'helpfully' confident about details it never actually saw.

tasks.scenario: Should you cache generated clinical notes by hashing the input transcript, the way you might cache chatbot replies?
options: Yes — identical-looking transcripts should return the cached note instantly, saving API cost
No — every encounter is for a different patient, so even a near-identical transcript must never return another patient's cached note
Yes, but only cache for 24 hours to limit the risk window
Only cache the Assessment and Plan sections, since those change least between patients
hints: Think about what 'near-identical transcript' actually means when two different patients describe similar symptoms in similar words.
Caching by content hash assumes the same input should always produce the same output — that assumption fails the moment two different patients are involved.
tasks.why: Caching is a content-reuse optimisation. In clinical documentation the content is never meant to be reused across patients, no matter how similar the wording looks. Any caching scheme here risks attaching one patient's note to another patient's record.

tasks.scenario: This call has no timeout — during a live consultation, a hung request blocks the doctor's screen indefinitely. Fix it.

checks.label: Sets an explicit timeout on the request

tasks.why: Without a timeout, a slow or hung network call can leave the doctor staring at a blank screen mid-consultation with no way to know if it's still working. A hard timeout with a clear fallback message keeps the workflow usable even when the API misbehaves.

tasks.scenario: Put these pipeline steps for processing one dictation in the correct order, top to bottom.
items: Validate the returned JSON has all required SOAP fields
Strip or tokenise direct identifiers from the raw transcript
Send the cleaned transcript to the model with the system prompt
Log the transcript reference, model version, and output together for audit
tasks.why: PII handling has to happen before anything leaves your infrastructure, so identifiers are stripped first. Only then does the cleaned text go to the model. The response is validated before it's trusted. Logging happens last, once you have a complete, validated record to log.

tasks.scenario: The transcript never mentions a specific medication dose, but the generated note includes one anyway. What does this tell you, and what should the system do?
options: It's fine — the dose is probably standard for that medication, so it's a reasonable default to include
This is a hallucination. The Plan section should say 'dose not stated' and the note should be flagged for the doctor to fill in, never auto-completed
Round the dose to the nearest common value and include it, since an approximate number is more useful than a blank field
Remove the medication mention entirely so there's no incorrect number anywhere in the note
hints: A dose that wasn't said is not a 'reasonable default' in a clinical record — it's an invented fact attached to a real patient.
The safest output isn't a guessed number or a deleted mention — it's an honest gap the doctor fills in themselves.
tasks.why: A model inventing a plausible-sounding dose is the single most dangerous failure mode of a clinical assistant — it looks exactly like a real clinical fact. The only safe behaviour is to surface the gap explicitly rather than fill it, guess at it, or hide it.

tasks.scenario: Add a guardrail rule to the system prompt that forbids the model from suggesting a diagnosis, and requires it to mark any field it's uncertain about as 'not stated' rather than leaving it blank or guessing.
tasks.placeholder: You are a clinical documentation assistant...

checks.label: Requires marking uncertain fields explicitly rather than guessing

tasks.why: Forbidding diagnosis keeps the assistant in a documentation role, not a clinical decision-making one. Requiring an explicit 'not stated' marker — instead of a blank field, which can be misread as 'nothing relevant happened' — keeps the gap visible to whoever reviews the note.

tasks.scenario: This code saves whatever the model returns directly to the patient record, with no check that the JSON is actually complete. Fix it.

checks.label: Checks the parsed result has all required fields before saving

tasks.why: A model can return technically valid JSON that's missing a required field (e.g. no Plan section). Saving it straight to the patient record without checking means an incomplete note can silently look complete. The save step needs to validate structure first and reject anything missing a required field.

tasks.scenario: A generated note is ready. What happens next, before it becomes part of the official patient record?
options: It's saved automatically — review would just slow doctors down, and the guardrails already catch the risky cases
The doctor reviews and explicitly signs off on the note — the AI drafts, the doctor remains responsible for what's recorded
It's saved automatically but flagged in a different colour so the doctor can review it later if they have time
A second AI model reviews the first model's output and approves or rejects it without human involvement
hints: Guardrails reduce risk, they don't remove responsibility for what ends up in a medical record.
Replacing one AI check with another AI check doesn't change who's accountable if something's wrong.
tasks.why: No amount of prompt engineering makes an AI legally or clinically responsible for a patient record. Mandatory human sign-off isn't a UX nicety here — it's the line between 'AI drafted this, doctor approved it' and 'AI wrote a medical record unsupervised', which is a fundamentally different (and far riskier) product.

tasks.scenario: Put these launch-readiness checks in the order you'd actually verify them before going live.
items: Confirm doctor sign-off cannot be bypassed anywhere in the UI
Confirm PII handling has been reviewed against the relevant regulatory framework
Run the assistant against real (anonymised) transcripts to check note quality
Set up monitoring so a spike in rejected/flagged notes is visible immediately
tasks.why: Regulatory and consent groundwork comes first — there's no point testing a system you're not legally allowed to run. Locking the sign-off step is the next non-negotiable before any output reaches a real workflow. Only then does quality testing make sense, with monitoring as the ongoing safety net once it's live.

tasks.scenario: Costs are climbing faster than expected. What's the biggest lever specific to this kind of tool?
options: Switch to a cheaper model regardless of accuracy impact, since cost matters most
Trim transcript noise (filler words, repeated phrasing) before it reaches the model — shorter input, same clinical content, lower cost per note
Reduce how often doctors are allowed to use the tool per day
Cache notes more aggressively across similar-sounding transcripts to cut API calls
hints: Cost here scales with transcript length, not user count — so the lever is in what you send, not how often.
One of these options reintroduces the exact cross-patient risk you ruled out earlier in this build.
tasks.why: Since cost tracks transcript length, cleaning filler and repetition out of the dictation before it's sent — without losing clinical content — directly reduces tokens per call without touching accuracy or reintroducing the caching risk already ruled out at the Senior level.
